questions.question_text: What must appear in all Montana real estate advertisements?
distractors: The salesperson's cell phone number
The brokerage street address
The MLS membership number
questions.explanation: Montana's advertising rule (ARM 24.210.428, effective August 2025) requires every advertisement by a licensee to include a truthful, accurate description of the property or service, the licensee's name, and a statement that the ad is made by a licensee or a brokerage company. Cell numbers, street addresses, and MLS numbers are not required.

questions.question_text: What elements are required in a Montana listing advertisement?
questions.correct_answer: An accurate property description and the licensee's name
distractors: Only the property address and price
The salesperson's personal phone number
The MLS number and listing date
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.428(1) requires a truthful, accurate, and detailed description of the property, the licensee's name, and a statement that the advertisement is made by the licensee or a brokerage company. Omitting the licensee identification violates the rule and is unprofessional conduct under ARM 24.210.641(1)(dd).

questions.question_text: A broker advertises under a DBA trade name. What additional name must appear?
distractors: The DBA registration number
The broker's personal home address
The state license number only
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.428(1)(b) requires the licensee's name in any advertising, so a broker using a DBA must still include the broker's own name and identify the brokerage. For franchise advertising, MCA 37-51-321(1)(a) requires the franchise name or logo to incorporate the broker's own name or the office's trade name.

questions.question_text: A Montana salesperson's ad shows her name and the property details, but nothing indicates the ad comes from a real estate licensee or brokerage. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, the ad must state it is by a licensee or brokerage
distractors: Yes, the licensee's name alone is sufficient
Yes, if the ad includes an MLS number
Only internet ads need that statement
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.428(1)(c) requires every ad to carry a statement that it is made by the licensee or a brokerage company (in internet ads, a link to this information may be used). Failing to identify licensee status in advertising is unprofessional conduct under ARM 24.210.641(1)(dd). Montana has no broker-name prominence requirement — the identification duty runs to the licensee and licensee status.

questions.question_text: A Montana salesperson includes 'Top Producer' in her ad. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if truthful and the ad identifies the licensee
distractors: No, personal titles are never allowed
Only if approved by the Board first
Yes, without any additional requirements
questions.explanation: Truthful designations are permitted; untruthful claims are intentionally misleading advertising (MCA 37-51-321(1)(a); 37-1-316(1)(g)). The ad must still meet ARM 24.210.428's identification requirements — the licensee's name and a licensee/brokerage statement. Note that using 'REALTOR®' without membership is itself a violation (ARM 24.210.641(1)(d)).

questions.question_text: What is a 'blind ad' in Montana real estate?
questions.correct_answer: An ad that hides the licensee's identity
distractors: An ad placed by an unlicensed person
An ad without a property address
An ad placed on social media only
questions.explanation: A blind ad conceals that the advertiser is a real estate licensee, making it look like a private-party ad. ARM 24.210.641(1)(dd) makes it unprofessional conduct to fail to disclose in advertising the licensee's name and that the advertisement is made by a real estate licensee or brokerage company.

questions.question_text: A licensee lists her own investment property on a classified site without disclosing her license status. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, she must disclose that she is licensed
distractors: Yes, personal property is always exempt
Yes, if she waives her commission
Only if the price is under $200,000
questions.explanation: Montana expressly permits licensees to sell their own property in for-sale-by-owner personal transactions (MCA 37-51-309(6)), but disclosure duties remain: failing to disclose identity as a real estate licensee at first contact is unprofessional conduct (ARM 24.210.641(1)(ff)), and any advertising by a licensee must include the licensee's name and licensee identification (ARM 24.210.428; 641(1)(dd)). Before entering the FSBO deal she must also disclose in writing that it is a personal transaction not involving her supervising broker (MCA 37-51-309(6)(c)).

questions.question_text: A salesperson advertises a listing with exaggerated claims about the property's income potential. Which rule does this violate?
distractors: The blind ad prohibition
The broker identification requirement
The team advertising disclosure rule
questions.explanation: Montana prohibits false or misleading advertising. Exaggerating a property's income potential constitutes misleading advertising that could deceive potential buyers into making uninformed decisions.

questions.question_text: A salesperson creates a flyer for a listing without obtaining the seller's authorization to advertise. What is the violation?
distractors: Using an unapproved flyer format
Failing to include the MLS number
Distributing materials on weekends
questions.explanation: Under MCA 37-51-321(2)(a), it is unlawful for a licensee to openly advertise property belonging to others unless the broker or salesperson has a signed listing agreement from the owner that is valid as of the date of the advertisement. Creating and distributing marketing materials without that authorization is a violation.

questions.question_text: A salesperson advertises another broker's listing on her website. What is required?
distractors: Only her own broker's name in the ad
Nothing, listings are public information
A written agreement with the seller
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.428(3) requires both the listing agent's and the property owner's consent to advertise another firm's listing (owner consent may come through the listing agreement, and MLS offers of cooperation can supply the listing agent's consent). The ad must also advise that the property is listed with another licensee or brokerage and identify the listing agent or brokerage company.

questions.question_text: A salesperson posts a property listing on social media. Do Montana advertising rules apply?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, all advertising rules apply equally
distractors: No, social media posts are informal
Only if the post is paid or boosted
Only if the post includes a price
questions.explanation: Montana's advertising rule applies to any form of advertising, including social media (ARM 24.210.301(1), 24.210.428). The licensee's name and the licensee/brokerage identification are required regardless of platform; in internet ads a link to the identification may be used.

questions.question_text: A team creates a website using only the team name as the domain. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the site identifies the licensee and brokerage
distractors: No, domains must use the brokerage name
Yes, domain names have no restrictions
Only if the team is independently licensed
questions.explanation: Montana imposes no domain-name or team-name restrictions. The website content must simply satisfy ARM 24.210.428(1): the licensee's name and a statement that the advertising is by a licensee or brokerage company — and a link to this identification is permitted for internet advertising.

questions.question_text: A brokerage website displays listings, and every page links to an 'About Us' page that names the licensee and brokerage. Is this sufficient?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, a link to the identification is permitted
distractors: No, identification must be repeated on every page
No, internet ads cannot rely on links
Only if the site displays fewer than ten listings
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.428(1)(c) provides that in an internet advertisement, a link to the licensee identification may be used, and 428(2)(b) allows banner ads to rely on a link to a web page with licensee identification. There is no requirement to repeat the identification on every page.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent uses an automated text system to send property alerts. Do advertising rules apply?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, all electronic ads must comply
distractors: No, texts are personal communications
Only if the texts include listing photos
Only if more than 100 texts are sent
questions.explanation: Property-alert texts promote real property and are 'advertising' under ARM 24.210.301(1), so the advertising rules apply. For instant messaging, ARM 24.210.428(2)(c) allows the licensee identification to be given via another format before licensable services are offered, rather than inside every text.

questions.question_text: How do Montana's advertising rules treat real estate team names?
questions.correct_answer: No special team rule exists; licensee identification still applies
distractors: Team names must be registered with the Board
A team name may replace all licensee identification
Teams must obtain a separate brokerage license
questions.explanation: Montana law contains no team-name registration or approval requirements. Any team advertising must simply satisfy ARM 24.210.428: the licensee's name and a statement that the ad is made by the licensee or a brokerage company.
